title: Topic 168: Why Players Join Losing Teams: The Underdog Effect
content:
# Topic 168: Why Players Join Losing Teams: The Underdog Effect

## Abstract

This paper delves into the perplexing, often irrational, human tendency to support, and even join, the long shot, the dark horse, and the perpetually losing team. We explore the "Underdog Effect," a phenomenon that defies conventional logic and economic principles, turning fans and players into willing participants in narratives of struggle and occasional, glorious, and statistically improbable triumph. Through a rigorous, albeit amusing, analysis of psychological theories, real-world case studies, and consumer behavior, we uncover the motivations behind this curious behavior. From the schadenfreude of watching a dynasty fall to the deep-seated human need for a just world, this paper explains why, for many, the victory of the downtrodden is the only victory that truly matters. We conclude that the Underdog Effect is not merely a quirk of sports fandom but a profound insight into the human condition, with significant implications for branding, marketing, and our understanding of ourselves.

## Introduction

In the grand casino of life, where the house usually wins, there exists a peculiar subset of gamblers who consistently place their bets on the horse with three legs, the boxer with a glass jaw, and the football team that considers a scoreless draw a moral victory. This paper is about them. It is an academic exploration into the heart of the underdog lover, the fan who scoffs at dynasties and cheers for the scrappy, perpetually outmatched team that probably, definitely, won't win. Why do we do it? Why do we emotionally invest in teams that bring us more heartache than joy? And, more puzzlingly, why do some athletes, at the peak of their careers, choose to sign with these very teams?

This phenomenon, which we shall refer to as the "Underdog Effect," is a fascinating wrinkle in human psychology. It’s the reason David vs. Goliath is a timeless story and not just a cautionary tale about the importance of wearing proper headgear in combat. It’s why the movie *Rocky* resonates more deeply than a documentary about a heavyweight champion who wins every fight by knockout in the first round. We are, it seems, suckers for a good struggle.

This paper will dissect the Underdog Effect from multiple angles. We will explore the psychological underpinnings of this behavior, from our innate sense of fairness to the vicarious thrill of an unexpected victory. We will examine how this effect manifests in the world of sports and gaming, with a special focus on the seemingly irrational decisions of players who choose to join losing teams. Finally, we will broaden our scope to consider the implications of the Underdog Effect on consumer behavior and branding, revealing how marketers can harness our love for the little guy to build powerful brand narratives. Prepare for a journey into the minds of those who believe that the bigger they are, the harder they fall, and that a single, hard-won victory is worth a thousand easy wins.

## Literature Review

Our fascination with the underdog is not just a matter of anecdotal observation; it is a well-documented psychological phenomenon. Researchers have been poking and prodding at this curious human trait for decades, and their findings are as entertaining as they are enlightening. A seminal study by Kim et al. (2008) found that individuals are overwhelmingly likely to root for a fictional team described as the underdog, even to the point of switching allegiances mid-series if the favorite suddenly finds itself on the ropes. This suggests that our support is not for a specific team, but for the *narrative* of the underdog itself.

But *why* do we love this narrative so much? One popular theory, as explored by Goldschmied (2015), is that it’s all about schadenfreude – the pleasure we derive from the misfortune of others. We resent the seemingly effortless success of top-dog teams like the New York Yankees or Manchester United, and we revel in their occasional, humbling defeats. However, the evidence for this is mixed. The same research shows that our support for the underdog wanes if their victory comes at too great a cost to the favorite, suggesting that our motivations are more complex than simple spite.

An alternative explanation lies in our innate desire for a just and equitable world. Vandello, Goldschmied, and Richards (2007) propose that we root for the underdog because their victory reaffirms our belief that success should be earned through effort and determination, not just inherited through superior resources. When a team with a shoestring budget topples a financial behemoth, it feels like a victory for fairness itself. This “just world” hypothesis is supported by findings that our underdog sympathies disappear when the “underdog” is revealed to have a hidden advantage, such as a massive, undisclosed payroll.

Finally, there is the theory of emotional hedging. As any long-suffering fan of a losing team will tell you, the pain of an expected loss is far more manageable than the agony of an unexpected one. By rooting for the underdog, we set ourselves up for a win-win emotional scenario: if they lose, it’s no big deal; if they win, the joy is magnified tenfold. While this theory has not been as extensively studied in the context of underdog fandom, it offers a compellingly rational explanation for a seemingly irrational behavior. In essence, rooting for the underdog is a low-risk, high-reward emotional investment.

## Analysis

The decision of a player to join a losing team is a particularly fascinating manifestation of the Underdog Effect. From a purely rational, economic perspective, it makes little sense. Why would a talented athlete, at the peak of their physical and financial powers, choose to sign with a team that offers a lower salary, a less-than-stellar supporting cast, and a statistically insignificant chance of winning a championship? The answer, it seems, lies in a potent cocktail of psychological motivations that transcend the simple logic of dollars and cents.

One of the most powerful of these motivations is the allure of the heroic narrative. A player who joins a winning team is simply another cog in a well-oiled machine. Their individual contribution, no matter how significant, is diluted by the team's pre-existing success. But a player who joins a losing team and turns them into a winner? That player becomes a legend. They are the savior, the messiah, the one who brought rain to a parched land. This narrative is incredibly seductive, not just to the fans, but to the players themselves. It offers a chance at a kind of sporting immortality that a simple championship ring cannot provide.

This desire for a heroic narrative is closely tied to the concept of self-efficacy. A player who believes in their own ability to effect change will be drawn to situations where their impact can be most dramatically demonstrated. A losing team is a blank canvas, an opportunity for a player to paint their masterpiece. This is not just about ego; it's about a deep-seated psychological need to prove one's own value and competence.

Of course, we cannot discount the role of financial incentives, albeit in a less direct form. While a losing team may not be able to offer the highest salary, they can offer something else: a bigger piece of the pie. A star player on a losing team is not just a player; they are the franchise. They are the face of the team, the name on the back of every jersey, the star of every commercial. This increased visibility can translate into lucrative endorsement deals and a personal brand that transcends the team's on-field performance. In a sense, the player is not just joining a team; they are acquiring a marketing platform.

Finally, there is the simple, undeniable, human desire to be loved. A player who joins a winning team is respected, admired, and perhaps even feared. But a player who joins a losing team and makes them a winner is *adored*. They are the prodigal son who returned to lead his people out of the wilderness. This deep, emotional connection with a fanbase is a powerful motivator, and it is a reward that a winning team, with its legions of bandwagon fans, can rarely offer.

## Real-World Examples

The annals of sports history are filled with examples of players who have embraced the underdog role, with varying degrees of success. Perhaps the most iconic example is LeBron James's return to the Cleveland Cavaliers in 2014. After winning two championships with the Miami Heat, a team he had joined as a free agent in a move that was widely criticized as taking the easy way out, James returned to his hometown team, a franchise that had been mired in mediocrity since his departure. His goal was explicit: to bring a championship to a city that had not celebrated a major sports title in over 50 years. It was a move straight out of a Hollywood script, and when he finally delivered on his promise in 2016, it was a moment of pure, unadulterated sporting catharsis.

In the world of gaming, we see a similar phenomenon. In massively multiplayer online games like *World of Warcraft*, players will often choose to join the faction that is outnumbered or has a lower win rate in player-versus-player combat. They do this not because they enjoy losing, but because the victories, when they do come, are all the more sweet. The sense of camaraderie and shared struggle within these underdog factions is often far stronger than in the dominant, overpopulated factions. It is a digital echo of the same tribal loyalty that binds fans to their perpetually losing sports teams.

Even in the world of professional wrestling, a sport that is entirely scripted, the underdog narrative is a powerful and enduring trope. Characters like Daniel Bryan, a wrestler who was consistently portrayed as being too small and too unconventional to be a top star, became one of the most popular performers in the history of the industry precisely because of his underdog status. The fans saw in him a reflection of their own struggles against a system that seemed designed to keep them down, and they rallied behind him with a passion that was anything but scripted.

## Discussion

The Underdog Effect is more than just a curious quirk of sports and gaming; it is a window into the very soul of the consumer. The same psychological principles that drive us to root for the losing team also shape our purchasing decisions and our brand loyalties. In a marketplace dominated by corporate Goliaths, the scrappy startup with a compelling underdog story can capture our hearts and our wallets.

This is the principle behind underdog branding, a marketing strategy that has been used to great effect by companies like Apple, which in its early days positioned itself as the creative, rebellious alternative to the monolithic IBM. It’s the reason we root for the local craft brewery against the multinational beer conglomerate, and the independent bookstore against the online retail giant. We are drawn to brands that embody the values we admire: passion, determination, and a willingness to challenge the status quo.

However, the Underdog Effect is a double-edged sword. A brand that builds its identity around its underdog status risks losing that identity if it becomes too successful. The moment the underdog becomes the top dog, the narrative is broken, and the brand must find a new story to tell. This is the challenge that has faced many successful startups, who have had to navigate the tricky transition from scrappy upstart to established market leader.

Furthermore, our love for the underdog is not unconditional. As the research shows, our support for the underdog is “a mile wide and an inch deep.” When the stakes are high, we tend to revert to the safe, reliable choice. We may root for the plucky independent filmmaker at the Oscars, but when it comes to our own retirement savings, we’re more likely to trust the established financial institution. This suggests that the Underdog Effect is most powerful in low-risk, emotionally driven decisions, and less so in high-risk, rational ones.

## Conclusion

In the final analysis, our love for the underdog is a reflection of our own hopes, fears, and aspirations. We see in the struggling team a mirror of our own battles, and in their improbable victories, a glimmer of hope for our own. The player who joins a losing team is not just making a career choice; they are making a statement. They are choosing the path of greatest resistance, the path of greatest potential reward, not in terms of money or fame, but in terms of narrative and legacy.

So the next time you see a fan passionately cheering for a team that has no chance of winning, or a player signing with a franchise that is a perennial cellar-dweller, do not dismiss them as irrational or misguided. They are simply playing a different game, a game where the score is not measured in wins and losses, but in moments of unexpected glory, in the shared struggle of a community, and in the timeless, enduring power of a good story. And in that game, the underdog is always the champion.
references:
1.  Goldschmied, N. (2015). The science of why we love to root for underdogs. *Vox*. Retrieved from https://www.vox.com/2015/3/20/8260445/underdogs-psychology
2.  Kim, J. H., Allison, S. T., Eylon, D., Goethals, G. R., Markus, M. J., Hindle, S. M., & McGuire, H. A. (2008). Rooting for (and then abandoning) the underdog. *Journal of Applied Social Psychology*, *38*(10), 2450-2473.
3.  Vandello, J. A., Goldschmied, N. P., & Richards, D. A. (2007). The appeal of the underdog. *Personality and Social Psychology Bulletin*, *33*(12), 1603-1616.
